Amazon CloudWatch Logs supports managed syslog ingestion
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon CloudWatch Logs, syslog ingestion, managed, TCP, RFC

Summary: Amazon CloudWatch Logs has launched managed syslog ingestion, allowing customers to send syslog messages from various devices like firewalls, routers, and Linux servers directly into CloudWatch Logs. This can be accomplished without the need to install or manage any agents, as users can configure their network devices to transmit syslog messages via TCP, TCP+TLS, or UDP directly to a VPC endpoint in their AWS account. The service supports multiple syslog formats, including RFC 5424, RFC 3164, and Cisco FTD/ASA, ensuring compatibility with diverse infrastructures. CloudWatch Logs automatically parses incoming syslog messages, extracting structured fields such as facility, severity, hostname, and application name, which simplifies the log management process and negates the need for custom parsing pipelines. This feature is particularly beneficial for security event investigations and troubleshooting, as it enables users to immediately query logs by severity or hostname using Logs Analytics. Overall, this new functionality centralizes log visibility, streamlines operational workflows, and reduces the logistical burden of maintaining log collection agents, and is available in most AWS regions, excluding the Middle East locations listed.

Amazon CloudWatch Logs supports managed syslog ingestion, enabling customers to send syslog messages from firewalls, routers, switches, and Linux servers directly into CloudWatch Logs.

With today's launch, customers can configure their network devices and servers to send syslog messages over TCP, TCP+TLS, or UDP to a VPC endpoint in their account - without installing or managing any agents. Amazon CloudWatch Logs supports RFC 5424, RFC 3164, and Cisco FTD/ASA syslog formats, making it compatible with a wide range of infrastructure. Amazon CloudWatch Logs automatically parses incoming syslog messages and extracts structured fields such as facility, severity, hostname, and application name, thereby eliminating the need for custom parsing pipelines. For example, customers can ingest syslog from their network firewalls and immediately query by severity or hostname using Logs Analytics to investigate security events or troubleshoot connectivity issues. This feature helps teams centralize infrastructure log visibility, simplify operational workflows, and reduce the overhead of deploying and maintaining log collection agents across distributed environments.

Available in all commercial AWS Regions except Middle East (UAE), Middle East (Bahrain), and Israel (Tel Aviv). To get started, see the Amazon CloudWatch Logs documentation.

item.title: SageMaker Notebook Instances now support G6e instance types
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon EC2, G6e instances, NVIDIA GPUs, SageMaker, generative AI

Summary: Amazon EC2 G6e instances are now generally available on SageMaker notebook instances. These instances are powered by up to 8 NVIDIA L40 Tensor Core GPUs, each equipped with 48 GB of memory, and utilize third-generation AMD EPYC processors. G6e instances offer a significant performance boost, delivering up to 2.5 times better performance compared to EC2 G5 instances. They are particularly suited for interactive model deployment testing and model training tasks, especially in the realm of generative AI fine-tuning. Users can leverage G6e instances for deploying large language models (LLMs) with parameters reaching up to 13 billion, as well as diffusion models for generating various forms of media such as images, videos, and audio. These instances are available in multiple regions, including the US East (N. Virginia and Ohio), US West (Oregon), Asia Pacific (Tokyo), Middle East (Dubai), and Europe (Frankfurt, Sweden, Spain). For further guidance, developers can refer to the developer guides to set up and utilize applications effectively on SageMaker notebooks.

We are pleased to announce general availability of Amazon EC2 G6e instances on SageMaker notebook instances.

Amazon EC2 G6e instances are powered by up to 8 NVIDIA L40s Tensor Core GPUs with 48 GB of memory per GPU and third generation AMD EPYC processors. G6e instances deliver up to 2.5x better performance compared to EC2 G5 instances. Customers can use G6e instances to interactively test model deployment and for interactive model training use cases such as generative AI fine-tuning. You can use G6e instances to deploy large language models (LLMs) with up to 13B parameters and diffusion models for generating images, video, and audio.

Amazon EC2 G6e instances are available on SageMaker notebook instances in the AWS US East (N. Virginia and Ohio), US West (Oregon), Asia Pacific (Tokyo), Middle East (Dubai) and Europe (Frankfurt, Sweden, Spain) regions.

Visit developer guides for instructions on setting up and using JupyterLab and CodeEditor applications on SageMaker Studio and SageMaker notebook instances.

item.title: Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory now supports cross-account access
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon Bedrock, cross-account access, memory resources, resource-based policies, multi-account architectures

Summary: Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory has introduced cross-account access, enabling multi-account architectures in which memory resources and consuming agents can span different AWS accounts. You can grant permissions for principals in one account to access memory data plane APIs for resources located in another account by using resource-based policies. Additionally, you can set up memory delivery destinations, such as Amazon S3, Amazon SNS, and Amazon Kinesis Data Streams, in different accounts. To initiate cross-account access, you need to attach a resource-based policy to your memory resource. Once this setup is complete, the principals in the consuming account can create events, write memory records, retrieve those records, and perform semantic searches by referencing the full memory ARN. Furthermore, cross-account delivery destinations allow your memory resource to deliver payloads and stream events to resources in other accounts, streamlining how data is managed across various AWS accounts. This feature is available in all regions where Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory is supported. For detailed instructions on getting started, refer to the Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Developer Guide.

Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory now enables cross-account access, allowing you to build multi-account architectures where memory resources and consuming agents span multiple AWS accounts. You can grant principals in one account permission to call memory data plane APIs against resources in another account using resource-based policies, and configure memory delivery destinations (Amazon S3, Amazon SNS, Amazon Kinesis Data Streams) that reside in a separate account.

Cross-account access is configured by attaching a resource-based policy to your memory resource. Once configured, principals in the consuming account can create events, write memory records, retrieve records, and perform semantic search by referencing the full memory ARN. Cross-account delivery destinations allow your memory resource to deliver payloads and stream events to S3 buckets, SNS topics, and Kinesis Data Streams in other accounts.

To get started, see Cross-account memory access in the Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Developer Guide. Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory cross-account access is available in all AWS Regions where Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Memory is supported.

item.title: AWS HealthOmics now supports ephemeral storage for private workflows
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS HealthOmics, ephemeral storage, bioinformatics, HIPAA-eligible, workflow tasks

Summary: AWS HealthOmics has introduced ephemeral storage for private workflows, providing dedicated scratch space that enhances performance and reduces costs for bioinformatics workloads. Each workflow task now has a dedicated local volume mounted at /tmp, allowing significant improvements in run times for tasks like genomic sequence alignment, BAM sorting, and variant calling. The service is HIPAA-eligible, catering to healthcare and life sciences sectors by enabling fully managed bioinformatics workflows. By default, each task comes with 16 GiB of free ephemeral storage, which can be increased up to 3,072 GiB per task using directives in WDL, Nextflow, or CWL workflow definitions. Users can enable this feature during execution via the StartRun API. All ephemeral volumes are encrypted and automatically deleted upon task completion. This storage option is available in AWS Regions such as US East (N. Virginia), US West (Oregon), Europe (Frankfurt, Ireland, London), Israel (Tel Aviv), and Asia Pacific (Singapore, Seoul). For further details on ephemeral storage and pricing, users are encouraged to visit the relevant AWS HealthOmics pages.

AWS HealthOmics adds ephemeral storage for private workflows, giving bioinformatics workloads dedicated scratch space that delivers more consistent run performance and lower costs. Each workflow task now receives a dedicated local volume mounted at /tmp, and workflows that generate significant scratch data, such as genomic sequence alignment, BAM sorting, and variant calling, can experience faster run times. AWS HealthOmics is a HIPAA-eligible service that helps healthcare and life sciences customers accelerate scientific breakthroughs with fully managed bioinformatics workflows.

With this launch, workflow tasks can write temporary data to their own local volume, keeping scratch I/O isolated from shared run storage that hosts the working directory. By default, each task includes 16 GiB of ephemeral storage at no additional charge. You can increase the amount of ephemeral storage allocated to individual tasks, up to a maximum of 3,072 GiB per task, using the appropriate directive in your WDL, Nextflow, or CWL workflow definition. You can enable ephemeral storage at runtime with the StartRun API. All ephemeral storage volumes are encrypted and deleted when a task terminates.

You can use ephemeral storage in all AWS Regions where AWS HealthOmics is available: US East (N. Virginia), US West (Oregon), Europe (Frankfurt, Ireland, London), Israel (Tel Aviv), and Asia Pacific (Singapore, Seoul). To learn more about ephemeral storage, visit the AWS HealthOmics User Guide. For more information on pricing, visit AWS HealthOmics pricing.

item.title: Amazon Cognito now supports customer managed key for encryption at rest
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon Cognito, customer managed keys, AWS KMS, encryption, user pools

Summary: Amazon Cognito has introduced support for customer managed keys within AWS Key Management Service (KMS) for encrypting user pool data at rest. By default, AWS uses owned keys, but customer managed keys provide organizations with complete control over these encryption keys, facilitating better adherence to data governance policies. Utilizing customer managed keys allows organizations to define specific policies, revoke access to encrypted data, and manage the lifecycle and permissions associated with the keys directly in AWS KMS. Users can either configure a customer managed key when creating a new user pool or update an existing one to use it. Additionally, AWS CloudTrail can be used to monitor and audit the usage of these keys, providing visibility into access patterns of identity data. These customer managed keys are available in user pools that fall under the Essentials and Plus tiers without incurring additional costs. To begin using customer managed keys, users can configure them using the AWS Management Console, AWS CLI, or AWS SDKs, with specific instructions available on the official AWS documentation site.

Amazon Cognito now supports customer managed keys in AWS Key Management Service (KMS) for encrypting user pool data at rest. While AWS owned keys are used by default to protect your data, customer managed keys give you full control over the encryption keys, helping you achieve your organization's data governance objectives. 

 

With customer managed keys, you can define organizational policies and revoke access to encrypted data by disabling or deleting your key. You create and manage the customer managed key lifecycle and usage permissions in AWS KMS. You can configure a customer managed key when creating a new user pool or update an existing user pool to use one. You can also use AWS CloudTrail to monitor and audit all usage of your customer managed keys, giving you visibility into when and how your identity data is accessed.

 

Customer managed keys are available in user pools in Essentials and Plus tiers at no additional costs. Standard AWS KMS charges apply. To get started, configure your customer managed keys using the AWS Management Console, AWS CLI, or AWS SDKs. Visit the developer guide for instructions.

item.title: Automated Reasoning checks in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails add new policy refinement workflows
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS, Amazon Bedrock, automated reasoning, guardrails, workflows

Summary: AWS has announced the introduction of new automated refinement workflows for Automated Reasoning checks in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails. These checks utilize formal logic to mathematically validate the accuracy of generative AI responses against user-defined policies, aiming to identify hallucinations and provide verifiable explanations. The quality of these validations hinges on how well a policy is articulated. The newly launched workflows are designed to assist customers in refining their policies with reduced manual effort, leading to enhanced reliability in Guardrail validation results. There are two key workflows introduced: the iterative policy improvement workflow allows customers with existing natural language tests to initiate an iterative refinement run, enabling the system to determine necessary changes for policy compliance. The ambiguity reduction workflow aids customers who face frequent ambiguous translation results by automatically refining variable descriptions and type definitions, thereby minimizing ambiguous translations. Both workflows can be accessed through the Amazon Bedrock APIs and the AWS Management Console, where users can initiate them by selecting "Refine policy" on the policy page. These features are available across all AWS Regions where Amazon Bedrock Guardrails are operational. To learn more, customers are encouraged to visit the official AWS documentation.

Today, AWS announces new automated refinement workflows for Automated Reasoning checks in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails. Automated Reasoning checks use formal logic to mathematically validate the accuracy of generative AI responses against a policy you define, helping detect hallucinations and provide verifiable explanations. The quality of validation results depends on how well a policy is defined. The new workflows help customers improve their policies with less manual effort, leading to more reliable Guardrail validation results.

The launch introduces two refinement workflows. With the iterative policy improvement workflow, customers who have created natural language tests for a policy can start an iterative refinement run, letting the system deduce the changes needed for the policy to pass those tests. With the ambiguity reduction workflow, customers who frequently encounter ambiguous translation results can run the resolve policy ambiguities workflow to automatically refine variable descriptions and type definitions, reducing how often ambiguous translations occur. Both workflows are available through the Amazon Bedrock APIs and in the AWS Management Console, where customers can start a workflow by choosing Refine policy on the policy page.

These workflows are available in all AWS Regions where Automated Reasoning checks in Amazon Bedrock Guardrails are available. To learn more, visit the Amazon Bedrock Guardrails product page and the Automated Reasoning checks User Guide.

item.title: Amazon CloudWatch launches OTel Container Insights for Amazon EKS
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: CloudWatch, EKS, metrics, dashboards, PromQL

Summary: CloudWatch OpenTelemetry (OTel) Container Insights for Amazon EKS collects infrastructure metrics at a 30-second granularity using open-source receivers like cAdvisor, Kube State Metrics, and NVIDIA DCGM. Each metric adheres to OpenTelemetry semantic conventions and includes Kubernetes labels, facilitating seamless correlation across nodes, pods, and workloads with a single PromQL query. Immediate visibility into cluster health, node performance, and pod-level resource usage is provided through pre-built dashboards. The CloudWatch PromQL endpoint enables direct connectivity to existing Prometheus and Grafana dashboards for easier integration. Users can enable this functionality via the EKS console, the CloudWatch Observability add-on (version 6.2.0 and above), Helm, or CloudFormation. Availability extends across all commercial AWS Regions, excluding the Middle East (UAE), Middle East (Bahrain), and Israel (Tel Aviv). For detailed pricing information, users can refer to Amazon CloudWatch, and to initiate setup, guidance is available in the OTel Container Insights documentation.

CloudWatch OTel Container Insights for Amazon EKS collects infrastructure metrics at 30-second granularity using open-source receivers including cAdvisor, Kube State Metrics, and NVIDIA DCGM. Each metric carries OpenTelemetry semantic conventions and Kubernetes labels, making it straightforward to correlate across nodes, pods, and workloads in a single PromQL query.

Pre-built dashboards give you immediate visibility into cluster health, node performance, and pod-level resource usage. The CloudWatch PromQL endpoint lets you connect existing Prometheus and Grafana dashboards directly to CloudWatch.

Enable it from the EKS console or via the CloudWatch Observability add-on (v6.2.0+), Helm, or CloudFormation.

Available in all commercial AWS Regions except Middle East (UAE), Middle East (Bahrain), and Israel (Tel Aviv). For pricing details, see the Amazon CloudWatch pricing page. To get started, see the OTel Container Insights documentation.

item.title: Claude Tag is now available in beta via Claude Enterprise in AWS Marketplace
item.content:encoded:
Claude Tag, Slack, AWS Marketplace, Claude Enterprise, beta

Summary: Anthropic has launched Claude Tag, a new feature that integrates the AI assistant Claude directly into communication channels, with an initial rollout in Slack. Currently, it is available in beta for AWS customers using Claude Enterprise through AWS Marketplace. Claude Tag enables teams to grant access to Claude in selected channels, allowing members to delegate tasks by tagging @Claude while focusing on their other responsibilities. The AI can build context by remembering relevant information from ongoing discussions and planning future tasks. For security and governance, Claude Tag operates with a unique identity per channel, featuring spend controls and an 'ambient mode' that is off by default. AWS Marketplace customers experience the same functionality and setup as first-party Claude Enterprise, with consumption-based pricing that monitors usage instead of headcount, providing visibility into organizational budgets. An admin can provision the agent identity in the Claude admin console within approximately one hour and scope it per channel. For more information, customers can refer to the AWS Marketplace for detailed guidelines.

Anthropic is launching Claude Tag — bringing Claude directly into the channels where your team already works, starting with Slack. Claude Tag is available today in beta to AWS customers who access Claude Enterprise through AWS Marketplace.

Claude Tag is a new way for teams to work with Claude. Grant Claude access to selected channels, and connect it to whichever tools, data—and even codebases—you choose.. It's multiplayer, so anyone in the channel can tag @Claude in, and delegate tasks to it while they focus on other work. Claude builds context by remembering relevant information from the channels it’s in, and can plan out tasks to complete in the future. And, for security and governance teams, Claude Tag operates under its own identity, scoped per channel, with spend controls and ambient mode off by default.

Getting started with Claude Enterprise on AWS Marketplace

The experience for Claude Enterprise in AWS Marketplace customers is identical to first-party Claude Enterprise: same setup, same capabilities, same controls. Consumption-based pricing tracks usage rather than headcount, with org-wide budget visibility and per-channel limits. Customers use their existing Claude Enterprise on AWS entitlement — an admin provisions the agent identity in the Claude admin console (approximately one hour) and scopes it per channel.

To learn more, see the Claude Enterprise in AWS Marketplace

item.title: Amazon OpenSearch Service now offers AI-assisted migrations
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Migration Assistant, Amazon OpenSearch Service, Apache Solr, Elasticsearch, live traffic migration

Summary: The Migration Assistant for Amazon OpenSearch Service has been enhanced to facilitate the transfer of self-managed Apache Solr, Elasticsearch, or OpenSearch deployments to OpenSearch Serverless or Managed Clusters. Launched in December 2023, this tool automates manual migration tasks, helping to simplify the traditionally complex and error-prone process of data migration. The new AI-assisted experience includes an agent-guided workflow that aids users in planning, deploying infrastructure, and executing both historical and live traffic migration more effectively. It now supports live traffic capture and replay specifically for Solr, providing an additional layer of convenience. The Migration Assistant ensures compatibility with various versions of Solr, Elasticsearch, and OpenSearch, allowing for a broader range of migrations. It is designed to support both OpenSearch Serverless and Managed Clusters, making it versatile for different deployment needs. Additionally, the tool is accessible in all commercial AWS Regions and AWS GovCloud (US) Regions where Amazon OpenSearch Service operates, ensuring widespread availability for users looking to streamline their migration process. For detailed version compatibility and further information, guidance is available through the official documentation.

Migration Assistant for Amazon OpenSearch Service now includes an AI-assisted experience that simplifies moving your self-managed Apache Solr, Elasticsearch, or OpenSearch deployments to OpenSearch Serverless or Managed Clusters. With the new assistant, you can use your preferred AI tools like Kiro, Claude Code, and others to plan a migration, deploy necessary infrastructure, and execute both historical and live traffic migration.

Migrations are often complex and require weeks of planning before any data movement can begin and even then, the process can be error-prone. We launched Migration Assistant in December 2023 to simplify migrating existing and live data from self-managed clusters to Amazon OpenSearch Service by automating manual migration tasks. The new AI-assisted experience takes this further: it provides an agent-guided workflow that helps you structure, execute, and validate your data migration faster and more reliably. Additionally, Migration Assistant for Amazon OpenSearch Service now supports live traffic capture and replay for Solr. To get started, see Migration Assistant documentation.

Migration Assistant supports migrations to OpenSearch Serverless and Managed Clusters from various Solr, Elasticsearch, and OpenSearch versions. For more details about the versions supported, see the documentation. Migration Assistant is available in all commercial AWS Regions and AWS GovCloud (US) Regions where Amazon OpenSearch Service is available.

item.title: Amazon SageMaker Studio notebooks now support G7e instance types
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000, G7e instances, EC2 UltraClusters, AI models, Elastic Fabric Adapter

Summary: The Amazon EC2 G7e instances are designed to feature up to 8 NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs, each equipped with 96 GB of memory. They utilize 5th Generation Intel Xeon processors, providing the capability to support up to 192 virtual CPUs (vCPUs). These instances facilitate impressive networking performance with up to 1600 Gbps of Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) bandwidth. G7e instances enhance multi-GPU workloads through NVIDIA GPUDirect Peer to Peer (P2P) technology, and for more extensive configurations, they also allow for NVIDIA GPUDirect Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) with EFAv4 in EC2 UltraClusters. This reduces latency for small-scale multi-node workloads. G7e instances are ideal for deploying large language models (LLMs), agentic AI models, multimodal generative AI models, and physical AI models. They provide top-tier performance for spatial computing tasks and applications that require combined graphics and AI processing capabilities. Currently, Amazon EC2 G7e instances are available for SageMaker Studio notebooks in the US East (N. Virginia and Ohio) and US West (Oregon) regions, and more information can be found in developer guides and on pricing pages.

Amazon G7e instances feature up to 8 NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs, with 96 GB of memory per GPU, and 5th Generation Intel Xeon processors. They support up to 192 virtual CPUs (vCPUs) and up to 1600 Gbps of Elastic Fabric Adapter networking bandwidth. G7e instances support NVIDIA GPUDirect Peer to Peer (P2P) that boosts performance for multi-GPU workloads. Multi-GPU G7e instances also support NVIDIA GPUDirect Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) with EFAv4 in EC2 UltraClusters, reducing latency for small-scale multi-node workloads. Customers can use G7e instances to deploy large language models (LLMs), agentic AI models, multimodal generative AI models, and physical AI models. G7e instances offer the highest performance for spatial computing workloads as well as workloads that require both graphics and AI processing capabilities.

Amazon EC2 G7e instances are available for SageMaker Studio notebooks in the AWS US East (N. Virginia and Ohio) and US West (Oregon) regions.

Visit developer guides for instructions on setting up and using JupyterLab and CodeEditor applications on SageMaker Studio. For pricing information on these instances, please visit our pricing page.

item.title: AWS Transform for migrations now supports all AWS commercial regions as migration targets
item.content:encoded:
AWS Transform for migrations now supports all AWS commercial regions as migration targets. A migration target region is the AWS region where migrated resources are deployed, including landing zones, network infrastructure, and server rehosting. Customers can now deploy workloads in any commercial region, making it easier to meet data residency requirements.

The new migration target regions are: US East (N. California), Africa (Cape Town), Asia Pacific (Bangkok), Asia Pacific (Hong Kong), Asia Pacific (Hyderabad), Asia Pacific (Jakarta), Asia Pacific (Kuala Lumpur), Asia Pacific (Melbourne), Asia Pacific (New Zealand), Asia Pacific (Taipei), Canada (Calgary), Europe (Milan), Europe (Spain), Europe (Zurich), Mexico (Querétaro) and Middle East (Tel Aviv).

Target region selection is available in the AWS Transform for migrations workflow. For the most up-to-date availability information, see the supported migration target region list.

item.title: MSK Replicator now supports mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication for replication from external Apache Kafka clusters to MSK Express brokers
item.content:encoded:
Amazon MSK Replicator now supports mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication for data replication from external Apache Kafka clusters - including on-premises, self-managed on AWS, or other cloud providers - to Amazon MSK Express brokers. With this capability, external Apache Kafka clusters configured with mTLS authentication can now use MSK Replicator to migrate workloads to MSK Express brokers, support disaster recovery by using MSK Express-based clusters as a failover or backup target, and enable data distribution across hybrid and multi-cloud environments.

MSK Replicator is a feature of Amazon MSK that automates data replication between Kafka clusters, eliminating the need to manage custom replication infrastructure or configure open-source tools. Previously, MSK Replicator supported SASL/SCRAM authentication only for connecting to external Apache Kafka clusters. With this launch, you can now also use mTLS authentication with MSK Replicator to replicate data from external Kafka clusters to Express brokers on Amazon MSK. Unlike self-managed replication tools, MSK Replicator lets you retain your original Kafka topic names during replication while automatically avoiding infinite replication loops. It also synchronizes consumer group offsets bidirectionally, enabling you to move producers and consumers across clusters independently, in any order, without coordination constraints or the risk of data loss.

This new capability is supported in all AWS Regions where MSK Express brokers are available. Visit the MSK Replicator documentation, product page, pricing page, and this AWS blog post to learn more.

Show HN: TikZ Editor – WYSIWYG editor for figures in LaTeX
item.content:encoded:
Hi all! TikZ is a widely-used LaTeX package for drawing figures in papers. It uses commands like \draw[->] (0,0) -- (1,2); to draw lines, shapes, text, etc. Academics usually code up their figures by hand, so there is lots of twiddling around with the coordinates and recompiling until things look nice. I guess it’s a bit like SVG, but it’s more code than markup, for example it has loops with \foreach.

I built an open-source WYSIWYG TikZ editor (available for web and desktop) that allows you to edit your TikZ source code visually by dragging and resizing elements. It simultaneously shows the source code and the rendered figure, and lets you edit either one while the two views stay in sync. I’m not aware of any other editors that are simultaneously source editors and WYSIWYG (even for editing SVG or HTML), and I’m quite pleased with how well the combination works.

The way the app is implemented is by parsing the TikZ code, and at all times keeping track of the exact source location of each object. Thereby, when a user drags an element to a new position, the app can override just the numbers in the coordinate without changing anything else in the code (such as line breaks or indentation).

This approach essentially required reimplementing a large fraction of TikZ, which is the kind of task that no human would ever want to do. I think building software that doesn’t exist yet because it would be impossibly tedious to code up is one of the great new possibilities thanks to coding agents, and it’s worth brainstorming for other examples. (This app was built almost entirely by Codex.)

Implementing the app came with lots of fun side quests, including building converters from SVG / pptx / ipe to TikZ, re-implementing the LaTeX hyphenation and line-breaking algorithm to support multi-line nodes, and making a color picker that uses the red!20!black color mixing notation used in LaTeX papers.

AWS HealthOmics now supports Nextflow profiles
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS HealthOmics, Nextflow profiles, workflow, HIPAA-eligible, bioinformatics

Summary: AWS HealthOmics has introduced support for Nextflow profiles, allowing customers to activate predefined execution settings during runtime. This feature enables the definition of reusable settings, facilitating a smoother transition between different execution scenarios without altering the core workflow source code. It supports HIPAA compliance, making it beneficial for healthcare and life sciences sectors focused on accelerating scientific research and bioinformatics workflows. With Nextflow profiles, users can segregate platform-specific configurations, such as resource limits and execution options, from the main workflow logic. This separation simplifies the switch between development and production settings, minimizing errors linked to manual edits and enhancing workflow portability. This is particularly advantageous when scaling from development to production environments. Additionally, users of nf-core workflows can activate pre-existing built-in and institutional profiles that come with those pipelines. Nextflow profiles can be specified in workflow runs across all AWS HealthOmics regions, including US East (N. Virginia), US West (Oregon), select European locations, Israel (Tel Aviv), and Asia Pacific (Singapore, Seoul). For further details, refer to the Nextflow Profiles section in the HealthOmics documentation.

AWS HealthOmics now supports Nextflow profiles, enabling customers to activate predefined execution settings at run time. Nextflow profiles allow customers to define reusable settings and select them at the point of execution, making it easy to switch between execution settings without modifying workflow source code. AWS HealthOmics is a HIPAA-eligible service that helps healthcare and life sciences customers accelerate scientific breakthroughs at scale with fully managed bioinformatics workflows.

With Nextflow profiles, you can cleanly separate platform-specific settings such as resource limits or execution options from core workflow logic. You can switch between development and production settings without creating separate workflow definitions. This reduces errors from manual edits, accelerates workflow portability, and saves time when scaling from development to production. If you use nf-core workflows, you can now activate the built-in and institutional profiles those pipelines already ship with.

You can now specify one or more Nextflow profiles in your workflow runs in all AWS HealthOmics Regions: US East (N. Virginia), US West (Oregon), Europe (Frankfurt, Ireland, London), Israel (Tel Aviv), and Asia Pacific (Singapore, Seoul). To learn more, visit the Nextflow Profiles section on HealthOmics Nextflow engine settings documentation.

item.title: AWS introduces Lambda MicroVMs for isolated execution of user and AI-generated code
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS, Lambda MicroVMs, isolation, execution environment, serverless

Summary: AWS has introduced Lambda MicroVMs, a new serverless compute primitive that offers VM-level isolation, rapid launch speeds, and state preservation for executing user-generated or AI-generated code. This solution enables developers to build multi-tenant applications where each user or job has a separate, isolated execution environment. Traditional options forced developers to compromise between security, speed, and state retention; however, Lambda MicroVMs combine these features without trade-offs. Built on Firecracker virtualization, which supports over 15 trillion monthly Lambda Function invocations, these MicroVMs allow for near-instant launch and suspension of execution for up to 8 hours. Developers can create MicroVM images from their Dockerfiles and launch MicroVMs with dedicated HTTPS URLs that are compatible with protocols like HTTP/2, gRPC, and WebSockets. Lambda MicroVMs are currently available in regions including US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon), Asia Pacific (Tokyo), and Europe (Ireland). Usage is billed based on baseline compute resources while the MicroVM is running, with additional charges only for resource use exceeding the baseline. For more details, visit the AWS website.

AWS introduces Lambda MicroVMs, a new serverless compute primitive that provides VM-level isolation, near-instant launch and resume speeds, and state preservation for executing user or AI-generated code. You can now give each user or job their own compute environment to securely run code without managing virtualization infrastructure or choosing between isolation, speed, and state retention.

Developers are increasingly building multi-tenant applications that execute code supplied by end users or AI for use cases such as interactive coding environments, data analytics platforms, coding assistants, and vulnerability scanning platforms. For these applications, developers need to allocate a separate, isolated execution environment per user or session to limit the impact of incorrect or malicious code on other concurrently running users or jobs. Previously, developers needed to choose between strong isolation, fast launch times, and state retention when building these applications. Starting today, Lambda MicroVMs provides you these capabilities without any trade-offs. You get VM-level isolation, near-instant launch speeds, and the ability to suspend and resume execution for up to 8 hours. Lambda MicroVMs is built on Firecracker virtualization, the technology powering more than 15 trillion monthly Lambda Function invocations. 

To get started, create a MicroVM image from your Dockerfile, then launch MicroVMs from that image. Give each user or job their own MicroVM with a dedicated HTTPS URL that supports popular connectivity protocols such as HTTP/2, gRPC, and WebSockets. 

Lambda MicroVMs is available today in the following AWS Regions: US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), US West (Oregon), Asia Pacific (Tokyo), and Europe (Ireland). To learn more, visit the AWS Lambda MicroVMs developer guide and the launch blog post. Get started with MicroVMs through the AWS Lambda console, AWS CloudFormation, AWS Cloud Development Kit, or use the Agent Toolkit for AWS with your preferred Agentic development tools. You pay for baseline compute resources while your MicroVM is running, and only for the active duration of additional resources consumed when your workload exceeds the baseline. To learn more about pricing, see Lambda MicroVMs pricing.

item.title: AWS Network Firewall updates default drop action for improved connection reliability
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS Network Firewall, application drop, stateful action, policies, TCP packets

Summary: AWS Network Firewall has updated its default stateful action for newly created firewall policies from "Application drop established (bidirectional)" to "Application drop established (server-directed only)." This change addresses significant issues by preventing the silent dropping of legitimate TCP packets like window updates, keep-alives, and resets, which previously led to intermittent connection failures that were challenging to diagnose. This safer default action enhances the reliability of network communications. Existing policies that require the previous setting for post-quantum cryptography (PQC) fragmented TLS handshakes can follow specific documentation guidelines to switch to the new default or can add the “to_server” flag to TCP drop rules to ensure legitimate flow control packets are not blocked. This update is universally applicable across all AWS Regions where AWS Network Firewall is available, making it easier for users to create new policies without needing to take further action. For those looking to implement these changes or learn more, detailed guidance is available in the AWS Network Firewall service documentation.

AWS Network Firewall now uses "Application drop established (server-directed only)" as the default stateful action for all newly created firewall policies, replacing the previous default of "Application drop established (bidirectional)" (formerly named "Application layer drop established"). No action is required to benefit from this change when creating new policies.

AWS Network Firewall is a managed service that lets you deploy network protections across your Amazon VPCs. Previously, the “Application drop established (bidirectional)” default could silently drop legitimate server-to-client TCP packets, such as window updates, keep-alives, and resets — causing intermittent connection failures that were difficult to diagnose. With the safer default now in place, new policies avoid this issue.

If your existing environment requires “Application drop established (bidirectional)” to support post-quantum cryptography (PQC) fragmented TLS handshakes, refer to our documentation for guidance on on switching to "Application drop established (server-directed only)" or adding the “to_server” flag to your TCP drop rules so legitimate flow control packets are not blocked.

This update is available in all AWS Regions where AWS Network Firewall is offered. To get started, see Managing evaluation order for Suricata compatible rules in the AWS Network Firewall service documentation.

item.title: AWS Batch now supports customer-ordered instance allocation strategies
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS Batch, BFPO, SCOP, instance types, compute environments

Summary: AWS Batch has introduced two new allocation strategies: Best Fit Progressive Ordered (BFPO) and Spot Capacity Optimized Prioritized (SCOP), enhancing user control over instance type prioritization within compute environments. These strategies allow users to manually define the order of instance types based on the performance characteristics specific to their workloads. To implement these features in AWS Batch, users need to specify the BEST_FIT_PROGRESSIVE_ORDERED strategy for on-demand compute environments and SPOT_CAPACITY_OPTIMIZED_PRIORITIZED for EC2 Spot compute environments. Additionally, an ordered list of instance types or families must be provided to utilize these strategies effectively. The new BFPO and SCOP allocation strategies are available through the AWS Batch API, specifically via the CreateComputeEnvironment or UpdateComputeEnvironment methods, as well as through the AWS Batch Management Console. These features are currently supported in all regions where AWS Batch is operational. For further details, users are encouraged to refer to the AWS documentation.

AWS Batch now offers the Best Fit Progressive Ordered (BFPO) and Spot Capacity Optimized Prioritized (SCOP) allocation strategies, giving you more control over instance type prioritization in your compute environments. BFPO and SCOP enable you to manually define instance type ordering based on your workload-specific performance characteristics.

To use these features in AWS Batch, specify BEST_FIT_PROGRESSIVE_ORDERED allocation strategy for your on-demand compute environments or SPOT_CAPACITY_OPTIMIZED_PRIORITIZED for your Amazon EC2 Spot compute environments and provide an ordered list of instance types or families. These features are available via the AWS Batch API (CreateComputeEnvironment or UpdateComputeEnvironment) or the AWS Batch Management Console.

BFPO and SCOP allocation strategies are supported today in all AWS Regions where AWS Batch is available. For more information, see the AWS Batch User Guide.

item.title: AWS IAM Identity Center now supports separate quotas for AWS accounts and applications
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS IAM Identity Center, quotas, accounts, applications, increase request

Summary: AWS IAM Identity Center now offers distinct quotas for configuring AWS accounts and applications within an instance. By default, users can set up to 7,000 AWS accounts and 7,000 applications independently, allowing for more flexible resource management, since utilizing one does not affect the capacity of the other. This is particularly beneficial for organizations managing a large number of AWS accounts, as they can onboard applications without impacting their account quota. For those needing higher limits, it is possible to request an increase through the AWS Service Quotas console. Customers who already possess higher limits will automatically receive the same expanded quotas for both accounts and applications, with no further action necessary. This update is applicable across all AWS Regions where IAM Identity Center is available, enhancing the platform’s usability and scalability for customers. To learn more about these updates and how to get started with IAM Identity Center, additional resources are available, providing guidance on the new capabilities and features.

AWS IAM Identity Center now supports separate quotas for the number of AWS accounts and applications that can be configured in an IAM Identity Center instance. By default, you can configure up to 7,000 AWS accounts and up to 7,000 applications independently, so that using more of one does not consume capacity from the other. Quotas can be further increased by submitting a quota increase request through AWS Service Quotas console.

Customers with existing higher limits are automatically granted the same limit for both accounts and applications, with no action required. Organizations managing thousands of AWS accounts can now onboard applications without consuming account quota capacity.

This update is available in all AWS Regions where IAM Identity Center is available.

To learn more, see Quotas for IAM Identity Center. Visit the IAM Identity Center product page to get started.

item.title: Amazon MSK now offers AI Agent Skills to help developers operate MSK efficiently and accelerate migrations to MSK
item.content:encoded:
**Keywords:** Amazon MSK, AI Agent Skills, Kafka, MSK Express, migration

Summary: Amazon MSK now features AI Agent Skills, enhancing AI coding assistants with expert guidance for managing Amazon MSK. These skills assist users with essential operational tasks, including troubleshooting, sizing, and configuring Kafka clusters, as well as monitoring and migrating from external Kafka clusters. By utilizing these skills, teams can maintain the health and performance of their clusters, and seamlessly migrate their workloads to MSK Express. MSK Express offers up to three times more throughput per broker, allows for 20 times faster scaling, and reduces recovery time by 90% compared to standard brokers running Apache Kafka. The AI Agent Skills streamline previously complex tasks into a more manageable experience, permitting developers to navigate them independently and efficiently. Integration is made easy through existing AI coding agents like Kiro, Claude Code, or Cursor. To start leveraging these capabilities, users configure their AI agent and can pose questions such as “What broker type and size should I use for my MSK cluster?” or “Is my Kafka cluster compatible with MSK Express?” This innovative approach enables developers to optimize their use of Amazon MSK effectively.

Amazon MSK now offers AI Agent Skills that give AI coding assistants expert, up-to-date guidance for operating Amazon MSK. The skills provide expert guidance for common operational tasks such as troubleshooting, sizing, configuring, monitoring, and migration from external Kafka clusters.

Teams can leverage these skills to keep their clusters healthy and performant, and to migrate their external Kafka workloads to MSK Express to take advantage of up to 3 times more throughput per broker, scale up to 20 times faster, and reduced recovery time by 90 percent as compared to Standard brokers running Apache Kafka. The skills turn tasks that once required specialized knowledge into a guided experience developers can complete quickly, on their own.

You can use the MSK skills with your existing AI coding agent - Kiro, Claude Code, or Cursor. To get started, configure the Agent Toolkit for AWS using the AWS CLI, then ask your coding agent a question, such as "What broker type and size should I use for my MSK cluster?" or "Is my Kafka cluster compatible with MSK Express?"

item.title: Introducing self-service lifecycle management capabilities for AWS Outposts
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS Outposts, self-service, configuration, renewal, decommissioning

Summary: AWS Outposts now enables self-service capabilities for managing the lifecycle of Outposts via the AWS Management Console, CLI, and API. Previously, customers needed to rely on AWS teams for various lifecycle management tasks, from initial evaluation to term completion. The introduction of a new configuration and quoting tool provides real-time cost estimates based on different payment options and term lengths, identifying account and regional constraints before order submission. Quotes can be generated in seconds and converted to orders directly within the console for new deployments or capacity expansions. Additionally, subscription details, including term dates and billing information, are now accessible in the console and programmatically, eliminating the necessity to contact AWS for this information. As the term end date approaches, customers can utilize self-service workflows to renew their Outpost with a new term and payment option or decommission it through a guided resource cleanup process. These enhancements are available in all commercial AWS Regions supporting AWS Outposts, making it easier for users to manage their Outposts effectively. To find out more, refer to the additional information provided by AWS.

AWS Outposts now provides self-service capabilities for configuration, quoting, ordering, subscription management, renewal, and decommissioning directly from the AWS Management Console, CLI, and API. Previously, customers relied on AWS teams for managing their Outposts lifecycle, from evaluation through end of term.

A new configuration and quoting tool generates real-time cost estimates across payment options and term lengths, and proactively surfaces account and regional constraints before order submission. Quotes are generated in seconds and can be converted to orders directly in the console, for both new deployments and capacity additions. Subscription details, including term dates and billing, are now available in the console and programmatically, eliminating the need to contact AWS for contract information. When your term approaches its end date, self-service workflows let you renew with a new term and payment option, or decommission your Outpost through a guided workflow that handles resource cleanup.

These features are available in all commercial AWS Regions that support AWS Outposts. To learn more, refer to the Launch Blog.

item.title: AWS Parallel Computing Service supports P6e-GB200 and P6e-GB300 UltraServers
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS, Parallel Computing Service, P6e-GB200, GPU workloads, Slurm

Summary: AWS Parallel Computing Service (PCS) has introduced support for Amazon EC2 P6e-GB200 and P6e-GB300 UltraServer instances, designed for extensive GPU workloads utilizing the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture in Slurm-managed clusters. Users can reserve these UltraServers via EC2 Capacity Blocks for Machine Learning (ML) and associate them with a PCS compute node group using an EC2 launch template. PCS simplifies the configuration of Slurm by automatically setting the appropriate topology plugin. The P6e-GB200 UltraServers provide access to 72 NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs within one NVLink domain, delivering a remarkable 360 petaflops of FP8 compute (without sparsity) and 13.4 TB of high bandwidth memory (HBM3e). The P6e-GB300 UltraServers enhance capabilities with 1.5 times the GPU memory and 1.5 times FP4 compute (without sparsity) compared to the P6e-GB200. AWS PCS is a managed service that streamlines the running and scaling of HPC workloads on AWS. It allows for the development of comprehensive, elastic environments that include compute, storage, networking, and visualization tools, while managing cluster operations and updates with built-in observability features. The P6e UltraServers are available in all AWS Regions where PCS and EC2 Capacity Blocks for UltraServers are offered.

AWS Parallel Computing Service (PCS) now supports Amazon EC2 P6e-GB200 and P6e-GB300 UltraServer instances, enabling customers to run large-scale GPU workloads using the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture within Slurm-managed clusters. You can reserve UltraServers through EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML, associate them with a PCS compute node group via an EC2 launch template, and PCS automatically configures Slurm with the correct topology plugin.

With P6e-GB200 UltraServers, you can access up to 72 NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs within one NVLink domain to use 360 petaflops of FP8 compute (without sparsity) and 13.4 TB of total high bandwidth memory (HBM3e). P6e-GB300 UltraServers provide 1.5x GPU memory and 1.5x FP4 compute (without sparsity) compared to P6e-GB200.

AWS PCS is a managed service that simplifies running and scaling HPC workloads on AWS using Slurm. You can build complete, elastic environments that integrate compute, storage, networking, and visualization tools, while the service handles cluster operations with managed updates and built-in observability features.

You can use P6e UltraServers with PCS in all AWS Regions where both PCS and EC2 Capacity Blocks for UltraServers are available. To learn more about P6e UltraServers, visit Amazon EC2 P6 instances. To reserve P6e UltraServers, contact your AWS sales representative. Read more about PCS support for P6e UltraServers in the PCS User Guide and make sure to set the right Permissions.

item.title: v16.3.0-canary.61
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: determinism test, Cache Components, React upgrade, runtime stage, App API routes

Summary: A fix was implemented for a failing determinism test related to an adapter. Documentation was improved by correcting a typo in the "slot-missing-default.mdx" file and clarifying that Cache components require a Node.js runtime. Error code attributes were correctly set for App API routes to better handle errors. A script was developed to write pnpm overrides for pnpm projects in pack/patch helpers. In the authentication documentation, Auth and streaming were added, and code fences and imports were corrected. Metadata image routes have been updated to be statically prerendered under Cache Components. React was upgraded to a new version. A codemod was introduced for cache components with an 'instant-false' approach and related to next-cache-components-adoption skills. Legacy PPR code paths were removed to streamline functionality. The `--session` flag was fixed for the next-dev-loop skill, along with clarifications for agent-browser bootstrap and a note on using `--headed` daemon. A global opt-in feature for the runtime stage of Cached Navigations was added. The turbopack module was enhanced by renaming the edge runtime to self-contained runtime and sorting modules by path rather than ID. Finally, the next-cache-components-adoption skill was fine-tuned to emphasize the app router requirement.

Misc Changes

  • [test] Fix failing determinism test using adapter: #95051
  • docs: fix typo in slot-missing-default.mdx: #94531
  • [otel] Correctly set error code attributes for App API routes: #94979
  • docs: Cache components requires Node.js runtime: #94897
  • [scripts] Write pnpm overrides for pnpm projects in pack/patch helpers: #94747
  • docs(authentication): add Auth and streaming, fix code fences and imports: #95056
  • Statically prerender metadata image routes under Cache Components: #94957
  • Upgrade React from b1786c31-20260618 to 247fbb45-20260622: #95063
  • codemod: cache-components-instant-false + next-cache-components-adoption skill: #94941
  • Remove legacy PPR codepaths: #94955
  • skill(next-dev-loop): fix --session flag, agent-browser bootstrap, --headed daemon note: #95005
  • Add a global opt-in for the runtime stage of Cached Navigations: #95064
  • [turbopack] Rename the edge runtime to the self-contained runtime: #94726
  • [turbopack] Sort modules in files by path not ID: #95065
  • skill(next-cache-components-adoption): tune skill to mention app router requirement: #95069

Credits

Huge thanks to @mischnic, @parkhojeong, @icyJoseph, @eps1lon, @aurorascharff, @unstubbable, @vercel-release-bot, and @sampoder for helping!

Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
item.content:encoded:
Oak is a version control system I've been working on designed for agents (https://oak.space). It improves the speed and context your agents need when working on serious projects. With virtual mounts, agents locally and in the cloud no longer need a full copy of a repo to get working. You can work on many tasks in parallel without needing to download everything or fight worktrees. Version control shouldn't waste you or your agents time. It should be fast, creative and fun to make things with agents.

Oak is still early in development. There's no Windows build and missing plenty of features (no CI, no issues, no comments). We still use GitHub Actions for building Oak now, but we've been fully bootstrapped on Oak with no Git backup for several months: https://oak.space/oak/oak.

Blog post: https://oak.space/blog#git-is-forever

Docs: https://oak.space/docs

item.title: Tell HN: Happy Fathers Day
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Soviet, Poland, uncle, rocket cars, Milicja

Summary: The story reflects a unique childhood experience under Soviet-controlled Poland, where the narrator's uncle took on the role of a father figure. They shared memorable moments, including a trip to a quarry where they enjoyed launching Estes-style rocket cars. This experience not only fostered creativity but also introduced the narrator to the joys of experimentation and engineering. Additionally, the uncle, who worked as an electrical engineer, managed to procure a Milicja (military police) siren from his job and creatively attached it to the back of the narrator's banana-style bike. His ability to make the siren function showcased his skills and ingenuity. This tale also touches on the broader theme of how innovation and resourcefulness during difficult times helped shape their identities. The gratitude expressed towards hackers indicates an appreciation for the individuals who inspire and influence others through their skills and creativity. Overall, the story highlights the impact of mentorship, the creativity born from constraint, and the enduring bond between family amidst the challenges of a politically tense environment.

My shortened story: due to factors in Soviet controlled Poland, my uncle played my dad role.

He took me to a quarry to fire Estes-style rocket cars, and all that.. he also managed to steal a Milicja (military police) siren from work, and put it on the back of my banana style bike. As an EE, he made it actually function.

Thanks to all the hackers who made us who we are.

I really like textboards, I think they are some kind superior than imageboard like reddit. I really love authless applications due its simplicity and privacy centric without complication, like strong cryptography and such.

I've been hosting a SchemeBBS instance for the last 2 years, but MIT scheme is not my favorite Lisp and I particularly think the ecosystem is pretty limited to the application grows with the features I want. The main different feature from schemeBBS are:

  • persistent preferences, like themes and default board
  • preview of image urls (that's kind crossing textboard concepts, but pretty useful — no image host anyway)
  • admin page for content moderation
  • search system

I would like feedback of any kind, thank you for your attention until here.

item.title: Show HN: StartupWiki – A Free Alternative to Crunchbase
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: StartupWiki, startup database, research, feedback, API

Summary: StartupWiki is a free, user-friendly startup database aimed at simplifying the process of discovering and researching early-stage companies. The creator was motivated by frustrations with conventional databases, which often require accounts or subscriptions and can feel cluttered. The vision is to create a platform resembling Wikipedia, where users can access information without barriers such as accounts or unnecessary metrics. Currently, the website features startup profiles, search and filtering options, company categorization, and an upcoming public API. The project is still in its early stages, and the creator is particularly interested in feedback from users regarding the types of information they seek when researching startups. Additionally, insights into features that are lacking in existing startup databases and potential use cases for the API are welcome. By focusing on user needs and simplifying access to startup information, StartupWiki aims to fill a niche in the startup research landscape. The creator encourages users to share their experiences and suggestions to help improve the platform.

I've been building StartupWiki, a free startup database designed to make it easier to discover and research companies.

The original motivation was frustration with how difficult it can be to find information on early-stage startups. Most databases need accounts, or subscriptions, ro just feel too cluttered. I wanted a website that felt like Wikipedia, no accounts, no subscriptions, no weird metrics, just go in, the info is on the page.

The project is still very early, but currently includes:

Startup profiles Search and filtering Company categorization Public API (in progress)

I'm especially interested in feedback on:

What information you look for when researching startups Features missing from existing startup databases API use cases

I'd love to hear feedback.

item.title: Announcing the general availability of a new AWS Local Zone in Hanoi, Vietnam
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS, Local Zone, Hanoi, infrastructure, Amazon S3

Summary: AWS has announced the general availability of a new Local Zone in Hanoi, Vietnam, marking it as one of the first in the Asia Pacific region. This Local Zone enhances AWS infrastructure by bringing services closer to end users, supporting requirements for data residency through localized storage and backup options. The Local Zone offers essential services like Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) and Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) Local Snapshots, which facilitate compliance with local data regulations. The deployment of AWS Local Zones enables single-digit millisecond latency for end-user workloads and aids in AI/ML inference workloads, making it easier for businesses to migrate and modernize legacy applications in the cloud. The Hanoi Local Zone provides support for various AWS services including Amazon EC2 with multiple instance types, Amazon S3 with One Zone-Infrequent Access, Amazon EBS volume types, and container orchestration services like Amazon ECS and EKS. To get started, users can enable the Hanoi Local Zone via the AWS Global View or through the API. Pricing information is available on the AWS website, where more details can also be found.

Today, AWS announces the general availability of a new Local Zone in Hanoi, Vietnam, bringing AWS infrastructure closer to end users. This new Local Zone is one of the first AWS Local Zones in the Asia Pacific with support for Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) and Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) Local Snapshots, enabling customers to meet data residency requirements by storing and backing up data locally.

AWS Local Zones are AWS infrastructure deployments that extend core services, such as compute, storage, networking, and other select services, closer to metropolitan areas worldwide. AWS Local Zones help you achieve single-digit millisecond latency for end-user workloads, meet data residency requirements, support AI/ML inference workloads, and accelerate migration and modernization of legacy applications to the cloud, all while maintaining consistent AWS APIs, tools, and services as AWS Regions. AWS Local Zones are available in more than 30 metropolitan areas worldwide. 

The Hanoi Local Zone supports Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) with C7i, M7i, and R7i instances, Amazon S3 with the One Zone-Infrequent Access storage class, Amazon EBS with Local Snapshots and volume types gp3, gp2, io1, sc1, and st1, Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS), Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS), Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (Amazon VPC), AWS Direct Connect, and Application Load Balancer. 

To get started, enable the Hanoi Local Zone (ap-southeast-1-han-1a) from the Regions and Zones tab in the AWS Global View or by using the ModifyAvailabilityZoneGroup API. For pricing information, visit the AWS Local Zones pricing page. To learn more, visit the AWS Local Zones overview page.

item.title: Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics now supports multilocation canaries
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon CloudWatch, Synthetics, multilocation canaries, AWS Regions, monitoring

Summary: Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics has introduced multilocation canaries, enabling developers and site reliability engineers to run the same canary across multiple AWS Regions from a single management point. Previously, monitoring applications from various geographic locations required managing separate canaries in each Region, which increased operational overhead and the risk of configuration drift. Multilocation canaries allow users to create and manage one primary canary, which CloudWatch Synthetics automatically replicates to additional selected Regions, consolidating run data, metrics, and artifacts in the primary Region. This feature ensures a consistent user experience globally, helps identify region-specific performance issues, and validates the functionality of third-party services like CDNs and payment processors. The replica canaries operate independently, providing resilient monitoring across various locations. Furthermore, users can set alarms to trigger only when issues are detected from multiple locations, boosting alert confidence and focusing efforts on genuinely impactful problems. Multilocation canaries are available in all AWS commercial Regions that support CloudWatch Synthetics. Existing single-region canaries can be upgraded to multilocation canaries by adding replica Regions without needing to recreate them. For additional details, users can refer to the AWS documentation.

Today, Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics announces support for multilocation canaries, allowing developers and site reliability engineers to run the same canary across multiple AWS Regions simultaneously from a single point of management. Previously, monitoring application availability from multiple geographic locations required creating and managing separate canaries in each Region, adding operational overhead and increasing the risk of configuration drift. With multilocation canaries, you create and manage a canary in one primary Region, and CloudWatch Synthetics automatically replicates it to the additional Regions you choose, consolidating all run data, metrics, and artifacts in the primary Region.

Multilocation canaries help you ensure consistent user experience worldwide, identify region-specific performance bottlenecks, and validate that third-party dependencies like CDNs and payment processors work across all locations. Replica canaries run independently, giving you resilient monitoring coverage across geographic locations. You can also configure alarms that activate only when issues are detected from multiple locations, increasing alert confidence and helping your team focus on real customer-impacting problems. Amazon CloudWatch Synthetics multilocation canaries are available in all AWS commercial Regions that support CloudWatch Synthetics. You can upgrade existing single-region canaries to multilocation by adding replica Regions without recreating them. For more information about regional availability, see the AWS Region table.

To learn more about CloudWatch Synthetics, see Using synthetic monitoring in the Amazon CloudWatch User Guide. To get started, visit the Amazon CloudWatch product page.

item.title: Amazon MSK Express brokers now support Intelligent Rebalancing on existing clusters
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon MSK, Intelligent Rebalancing, Express brokers, Provisioned clusters, automatic partition balancing

Summary: Amazon MSK now offers Intelligent Rebalancing on all existing Provisioned clusters with Express brokers, previously limited to newly created clusters. This feature enhances the automation of partition balancing, allowing customers to scale their Express-based clusters up or down effortlessly. Intelligent Rebalancing optimizes resource utilization for better performance in MSK Express-based clusters, eliminating the need for manual partition management or reliance on third-party tools. It operates up to 180 times faster than Standard brokers, ensuring quick adjustments. Continuous monitoring of clusters for resource imbalance or overload is conducted using Amazon MSK's intelligent defaults to maximize performance. If any issues are detected, brokers are efficiently scaled without compromising cluster availability, enabling uninterrupted data production and consumption. This enhancement is now accessible in all AWS Regions that support Express brokers, providing a valuable resource for businesses looking to streamline their Kafka operations. For more detailed information, customers are encouraged to refer to the official documentation.

Amazon MSK Provisioned clusters with Express brokers now support Intelligent Rebalancing on all existing clusters, at no additional cost. Previously available only on newly created clusters, Intelligent Rebalancing is now available on all MSK Provisioned clusters running Express brokers, making it effortless for customers to benefit from automatic partition balancing when scaling their Express-based clusters up or down.

Intelligent Rebalancing maximizes the capacity utilization of MSK Express-based clusters by optimally rebalancing Kafka resources for better performance, eliminating the need for customers to manage partitions themselves or via third-party tools. Intelligent Rebalancing performs these operations up to 180 times faster compared to Standard brokers. Clusters are continuously monitored for resource imbalance or overload based on intelligent Amazon MSK defaults to maximize cluster performance. When required, brokers are efficiently scaled without affecting cluster availability for clients to produce and consume data.

Intelligent Rebalancing is now available on all MSK Provisioned clusters with Express brokers in all AWS Regions where Express brokers are available. To learn more, see the Amazon MSK Developer Guide.

item.title: Amazon ECS announces faster service auto scaling
item.content:encoded:
High Resolution, Metrics, Auto Scaling, CPU, Memory

Summary: Amazon ECS service auto scaling has improved its response time to load changes with the introduction of high-resolution (20-second) metrics and optimization in metric publishing. Benchmark tests by AWS showed a significant decrease in the time needed to trigger scale-out, reducing it from 363 seconds to 86 seconds (a 76% improvement), while the total time to scale and provision new tasks improved from 386 seconds to 109 seconds (a 72% improvement). This enhancement allows users to lower baseline capacity and decrease compute costs without compromising service reliability and performance during fluctuating workload demands. The service now offers comprehensive scaling policies, including predictive scaling for recurring traffic, scheduled scaling for planned events, and target tracking for dynamic scaling based on real-time metrics. Newly supported are target tracking policies for CPU and memory utilization, which can utilize the high-resolution 20-second metrics or a default 60-second resolution for quicker scaling detection. Users can configure this through the AWS Console, CLI, CloudFormation, or SDKs when creating or updating their ECS service. This feature is available in all AWS commercial and GovCloud regions across all ECS compute options, including AWS Fargate and Amazon EC2. High-resolution metrics will incur standard CloudWatch charges.

Amazon ECS service auto scaling now detects and responds to load changes faster with support for high resolution (20-second) metrics and metric publishing optimizations. In AWS benchmarking tests, time to trigger scale-out improved from 363 seconds to 86 seconds (76% faster, 4.2x), and total time to scale and provision new tasks improved from 386 seconds to 109 seconds (72% faster, 3.5x). Faster service auto scaling also enables you to reduce baseline capacity and lower compute costs while maintaining service reliability and performance as workload demand fluctuates.

Amazon ECS service auto scaling automatically adjusts task counts to meet workload demand with comprehensive scaling policies, including predictive scaling for recurring traffic patterns, scheduled scaling for planned events, and target tracking to scale dynamically on real-time metrics. With today's launch, target tracking policies for CPU and memory utilization now support 20-second metric resolution, in addition to the default 60-second resolution, for faster scaling signal detection. To get started, use the AWS Console, CLI, CloudFormation, or AWS SDKs to configure 20-second resolution for CPU or memory utilization metrics when creating or updating your ECS service, then configure a target tracking policy selecting the corresponding high-resolution predefined metric.

This feature is available in all AWS commercial and AWS GovCloud (US) Regions, across all ECS compute options: AWS Fargate, Amazon ECS Managed Instances, and Amazon EC2. High-resolution metrics are subject to standard CloudWatch charges; for a pricing example, see Amazon CloudWatch pricing. To learn more, see our documentation and the launch blog post.

item.title: Amazon EC2 G7 instances are now generally available
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: AWS, EC2 G7, NVIDIA RTX PRO, AI inference, On-Demand Instances

Summary: Today, AWS announced the general availability of Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) G7 instances, powered by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs. These G7 instances provide significant enhancements, delivering up to 4.6 times AI inference performance and up to 2.1 times graphics performance compared to the previous G6 instances. They are particularly suited for various AI inference workloads, including language translation, image analysis, speech recognition, and recommender systems. Additionally, G7 instances are optimized for graphics-intensive tasks such as real-time rendering of cinematic-quality graphics, game streaming, and large-scale data analytics. Each G7 instance can host up to 8 NVIDIA GPUs, with each GPU featuring 32 GB of memory, custom Intel Xeon processors, and the capability of up to 700 Gbps networking bandwidth via Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA). Users can start utilizing G7 instances today in two AWS Regions: US East (Ohio) and US West (Oregon). They can be purchased as On-Demand Instances, part of Savings Plans, or as Spot Instances. Users can visit the AWS website, CLI, and SDKs for more information.

Today, AWS announces the general availability of Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) G7 instances, accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs. G7 instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference performance and up to 2.1x graphics performance compared to G6.

You can use G7 instances for AI inference workloads such as language translation, video and image analysis, speech recognition, and recommender systems. Additionally, G7 instances also accelerate graphics workloads such as creating and rendering real-time, cinematic-quality graphics, and game streaming, as well as data analytics workloads such as large-scale data processing pipelines. G7 instances feature up to 8 NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs with 32 GB of memory per GPU, custom Intel Xeon 6 processors, and up to 700 Gbps of Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFA) networking bandwidth.

You can start using Amazon EC2 G7 instances today in two AWS Regions: US East (Ohio) and US West (Oregon). You can purchase G7 instances as On-Demand Instances, as part of Savings Plans, or Spot Instances. 

To get started, visit the AWS Management Console, AWS Command Line Interface (CLI), and AWS SDKs. To learn more, visit this blog post and the G7 instance page.

item.title: Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ now supports private networking connectivity
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Amazon MQ, RabbitMQ, private networking, VPC, security compliance

Summary: Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ now enables private networking, allowing brokers to connect to private resources within your VPC without exposing them to the public internet. This enhancement is crucial for meeting various security and compliance requirements when brokers need to access private identity providers, other Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ brokers, or self-hosted RabbitMQ brokers. Previously, establishing such connectivity for RabbitMQ Federation, Shovel, or authentication necessitated workarounds involving Network Load Balancers and NAT Gateways. This new functionality uses Amazon VPC Lattice, AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM), and AWS PrivateLink while managing the underlying infrastructure automatically. To implement private networking, users need to create an Amazon VPC Lattice resource gateway, package the resource configurations into an AWS RAM resource share, and associate it with their broker. It's important to note that private networking is currently available only for Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ brokers and is accessible in all regions where Amazon MQ is available. For more detailed guidance on this feature, consult the Amazon MQ Developer Guide and additional resources.

Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ now supports private networking, enabling your brokers to connect to private resources in your VPC without exposing those resources publicly.. This helps you meet your security and compliance requirements when your brokers need to reach private identity providers (such as LDAP and OAuth 2.0), other Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ brokers, or self-hosted RabbitMQ brokers. Previously, this connectivity for RabbitMQ Federation, Shovel, or authentication required Network Load Balancer and NAT Gateway workarounds.

Amazon MQ establishes this connectivity using Amazon VPC Lattice, AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM), and AWS PrivateLink, and manages the underlying infrastructure on your behalf. To get started, create a VPC Lattice resource gateway, package your resource configurations into an AWS RAM resource share, and associate it with your broker.

Private networking is available only for Amazon MQ for RabbitMQ brokers, in all AWS Regions where Amazon VPC Lattice is available. To learn more, see Private networking in the Amazon MQ Developer Guide and the Amazon MQ pricing page.

item.title: Nested virtualization is now available on additional Intel platforms and US Gov Cloud regions
item.content:encoded:
Keywords: Nested virtualization, Intel platforms, US GovCloud, KVM, Hyper-V

Summary: Starting today, nested virtualization is being expanded to additional Intel platforms and regions, including C7i, R7i, M7i, C8id, R8id, M8id, and many others. This builds upon the existing support already available on C8i, M8i, and R8i instances. Additionally, the feature is now accessible in US GovCloud (US-East) and US GovCloud (US-West), along with all commercial regions. Nested virtualization allows customers to create nested environments by running KVM or Hyper-V on virtual EC2 instances. This capability opens doors for various use cases, such as running mobile application emulators, simulating in-vehicle hardware for automotive applications, and utilizing the Windows Subsystem for Linux on Windows workstations. As businesses increasingly seek to optimize their virtualization needs, this expanded support represents a significant enhancement.
